data.title: U.S. rare earth miners surge as China tightens export controls
data.description:
Shares of American rare-earth mining companies extended their rally Thursday after Beijing unveiled new restrictions on exports, fueling expectations that U.S. producers will see increased demand and support from Washington.

MP Materials (MP) jumped nearly 11%, USA Rare Earth (USAR) rose more than 20%, and Ramaco Resources (METC) (METCB) gained 14% before giving back some of the gains. The sector has been climbing since early July, when the U.S. government disclosed plans to buy a stake in MP Materials. Both USA Rare Earth and Ramaco have since tripled in value.

China’s Ministry of Commerce announced that exporters will now need licenses for products containing even small amounts of certain rare earths. Analysts warn that such measures could disrupt global supply chains given China’s dominance, accounting for about 90% of downstream rare-earth processing and products, Bloomberg News reported.

TD Cowen’s David Deckelbaum said the move reinforces the investment case for U.S. rare-earth companies, calling the domestic supply chain “a compelling place for capital right now.” He also noted that tightening fundamentals and growing federal involvement should continue to propel the sector.

The White House added momentum earlier this week by revealing that it would purchase a 10% stake in Canada’s Trilogy Metals (TMQ) (TMQ:CA), expanding its holdings in companies linked to critical minerals deemed vital to U.S. security interests. Rare earths are a key component in advanced weapons systems.

data.title: Fed Governor Barr urges caution in adjusting rates due to continued uncertainty
data.description:
Federal Reserve Governor Michael Barr said on Thursday that he's skeptical that the Federal Open Market Committee should "fully 'look through' higher inflation from import tariffs."

The series of recent tariff developments have not been predictable. "At some point, businesses and consumers could start to make pricing, spending, and wage decisions based on their belief in higher future inflation, thereby driving a cycle of persistence," he said in prepared text for a speech at the Economic Club of Minnesota.

As a result, "common sense would indicate that when there is a lot of uncertainty, one should move cautiously." 

In mid-September, the FOMC reduced its policy rate by 25 basis points to 4.00%-4.25%, a move that he supported. "Monetary policy was and remains modestly restrictive, so it seemed to me appropriate to move the rate a bit closer toward neutral, pending more data and further developments on the economy, the forecast, and the balance of risks," he said.

Barr cited the "Brainard Principle" for his careful approach to future moves. The principle "holds that when there is considerable uncertainty about the consequences of a policy action, the recommended course is to move more gradually than would otherwise be the case."

"I believe that principle applies now, and that the FOMC should be cautious about adjusting policy so that we can gather further data, update our forecasts, and better assess the balance of risks," Barr said.

If inflation drifts up further, it may be necessary to keep policy "at least modestly restrictive for longer," he said.  However, "if we see heightened risks in the labor market, then we may need to move more quickly to ease policy."

Developing... check back for updates.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Microsoft Azure deploys first large-scale cluster of Nvidia GB300 for OpenAI workloads
data.description:
Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) Azure has deployed its first large-scale cluster of Nvidia's (NASDAQ:NVDA) GB300 NVL72 systems, which feature more than 4,600 Blackwell Ultra GPUs connected with the Quantum-X800 Infiniband networking platform.

"First of many as we scale to hundreds of thousands of GB300s across our DCs, and rethink every layer of the stack across silicon, systems, and software to support next gen AI workloads," said Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella in a post on X on Thursday. 

"This co-engineered system delivers the world's first at-scale GB300 production cluster, providing the supercomputing engine needed for OpenAI to serve multitrillion-parameter models," said Ian Buck, Nvidia's vice president of Hyperscale and High-performance Computing. "This sets the definitive new standard for accelerated computing."

"Our collaboration helps ensure customers like OpenAI can deploy next-generation infrastructure at unprecedented scale and speed," added Nidhi Chappell, corporate vice president of Microsoft Azure AI Infrastructure.

The system provides 37 terabytes of fast memory and 1.44 exaflops of FP4 Tensor Core performance per VM, creating a large, unified memory space needed for reasoning models, agentic AI systems and multi-modal generative AI.

The announcement today tightens the bond between Nvidia, Microsoft and OpenAI. Last month, Nvidia revealed its plans to invest up to $100B in OpenAI to build and deploy at least 10 gigawatts of AI data centers with Nvidia systems.

Nvidia shares edged up 2% by afternoon trading on Thursday, reaching a new intraday high.

Microsoft continues to invest heavily in its data center footprint. The tech giant reported capital expenditures of $88.7B in fiscal year 2025, which ended June 30. During the first quarter of fiscal 2026, it plans to spend $30B, which is about a 50% year-over-year increase.

data.title: GoodRx spikes after report on plans to join TrumpRx
data.description:
GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX) shares added ~17% on Thursday after Reuters reported that the drug savings platform, along with a trade group representing pharmacy operators such as Costco (COST), is in talks with the White House about joining the government’s TrumpRx website.

Last week, President Donald Trump unveiled the direct-to-consumer site expected to launch next year, with Pfizer (PFE) agreeing to initially offer four of its brand-name drugs to cash-paying customers at sharp discounts.  

Announcing the deal at the Oval Office on Tuesday, Trump said other pharma companies are also expected to join the initiative, which comes under his most-favored-nation (MFN) pricing policy.

GoodRx (NASDAQ:GDRX) CEO Wendy Barnes noted that "if we include the pharma direct programs, we also have to find a way to convey competitive cash pricing at retail pharmacies."

According to Reuters, the National Community Pharmacists Association and the National Association of Chain Drug Stores, which represent pharmacy operators such as Costco (COST), have said they were also in discussions with the Trump administration. CVS Health (CVS), one of the largest pharmacy operators in the U.S., exited the National Association of Chain Drug Stores in 2022.

"As long as drugmakers deliver cost savings for American patients through TrumpRx, how they do so is irrelevant," White House spokesman Kush Desai noted.

data.title: Delta updates full-year EPS outlook to $6 and targets $3.5B–$4B free cash flow as premium demand accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Delta Air Lines (DAL) Q3 2025

Management View

  • CEO Ed Bastian reported "Delta's revenue growth and earnings came in at the top end of our expectations, delivering performance that we anticipate will lead the industry across all key financial measures" and highlighted revenue growth of 4%, pretax income of $1.5 billion, and earnings of $1.71 per share with an 11.2% operating margin. Bastian emphasized the company's focus on premium, corporate, and loyalty segments and noted "free cash was $830 million, bringing our year-to-date free cash flow to $2.8 billion."
  • Bastian stated, "Our fundamentals are improving and the positive momentum is continuing. Since July, travel demand has strengthened, led by a rebound in business travel, which was up high single digits in the quarter."
  • Bastian announced, "We expect to deliver a double-digit operating margin again in the December quarter with earnings comparable to what we earned in the September quarter. This would be at or above our all-time fourth quarter earnings performance."
  • For the full year, Bastian set the earnings outlook at "approximately $6 per share, which is in the upper half of our July guidance range" and updated free cash flow guidance to "$3.5 billion to $4 billion, growing our cash generation over last year."
  • President Glen Hauenstein confirmed, "For the September quarter, revenue increased 4.1% year-over-year to $15.2 billion, a third quarter record and ahead of our guidance as momentum built through the quarter."
  • Hauenstein noted, "Diverse high-margin revenue streams grew double digits year-over-year and contributed 60% of total revenue. Within that, premium revenue grew 9%... Loyalty revenue improved 9% and travel adjacent products grew mid-teens." He added, "Remuneration from American Express increased 12% over prior year to $2 billion in the quarter, keeping us on track to deliver over $8 billion this year."
  • CFO Daniel Janki said, "Nonfuel unit cost growth was approximately flat to prior year, bringing the year-to-date nonfuel unit cost growth to less than 2%, consistent with our low single-digit guidance at the start of the year, even as we've reduced capacity after the summer peak to align to demand."
  • Janki highlighted a successful repricing of the SkyMiles term loan, reducing the rate by 225 basis points and gross leverage ending the quarter at 2.4x.

Outlook

  • Management projects "revenue growth of 2% to 4% year-over-year" in the December quarter with "fourth quarter earnings of $1.60 to $1.90 per share and an operating margin of 10.5% to 12%." For the full year, Delta's guidance is "earnings per share of approximately $6, in the upper half of our guidance range we provided in July."
  • Free cash flow guidance is updated to "$3.5 billion to $4 billion."
  • Hauenstein expressed optimism about continued top line growth and margin expansion into 2026.

Financial Results

  • Delta reported record third quarter revenue of $15.2 billion, operating margin of 11.2%, and earnings of $1.71 per share.
  • Free cash flow for the quarter was $830 million, with year-to-date free cash flow at $2.8 billion.
  • Nonfuel unit cost growth was flat year-over-year, and operating cash flow for the quarter was $1.8 billion.
  • Remuneration from American Express reached $2 billion in the quarter.

Q&A

  • Duane Pfennigwerth, Evercore ISI: Asked about drivers of cash flow improvement. Janki responded that "the underlying improvement to offset that is coming out of working capital. We built up a lot of just... excess as we are rebuilding the airline. And now is our time as we drive efficiency to work that off, and you're seeing that in working capital."
  • Thomas Fitzgerald, TD Cowen: Requested details on domestic market improvements and premium seat mix. Hauenstein explained the company's exposure to higher-income households has enhanced its relative position. On premium seat mix, "most of our growth, if not almost all of it, will be in the premium sectors."
  • Catherine O'Brien, Goldman Sachs: Asked about efficiency gains and domestic Main Cabin inflection. Janki stated efficiency efforts are "in the very, very early innings of the unlock, and we have years of that in front of us."
  • Jamie Baker, JPMorgan: Queried about premium revenue drivers and overlap with corporate. Hauenstein said, "premium products used to be loss leaders and now they're the highest margin products... the best margins are in the most premium products."
  • Andrew Didora, BofA Securities: Asked about Atlantic RASM decline. Hauenstein described Q3 as "clearly disappointing" and outlined adjustments for next year, including booking curve and capacity changes.
  • Michael Linenberg, Deutsche Bank: Inquired on government shutdown impact, with Hauenstein stating it's "less than $1 million a day now."
  • Sheila Kahyaoglu, Jefferies: Sought Atlantic capacity outlook. Hauenstein expects "low single digits" capacity growth in peak months next year.
  • Savanthi Syth, Raymond James: Asked about Latin America. Hauenstein said long haul is "very solid" and short haul is "a mixed bag."
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Questioned Q4 earnings seasonality. Hauenstein indicated Q4 strength is driven by "strong premium demands and corporate travel in season."
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: Probed on sustainable loyalty and premium trends. Hauenstein pointed to "record number of premium card holders... their spend is a multiple of what our base member card spend is."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a positive to slightly positive tone, seeking clarity around sustainability of premium growth, margins, and improvements in Main Cabin and international trends. They pressed on forward momentum in corporate travel and implications of industry supply rationalization.
  • Management's tone was confident and optimistic in prepared remarks, emphasizing strong execution and improved fundamentals. In Q&A, management remained candid, acknowledging prior challenges (e.g., Atlantic RASM) while reiterating commitment to margin and premium growth. Confidence was reinforced with phrases like "I'm more optimistic than ever about Delta's future" (Bastian).
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management’s tone shifted further toward optimism, citing stronger demand recovery and improved Main Cabin trends. Analysts’ tone was less focused on risk and more on growth levers.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for full year EPS increased to approximately $6 from the previous range of $5.25 to $6.25. Free cash flow guidance also moved to $3.5 billion–$4 billion versus the previous $3 billion–$4 billion range.
  • Premium revenue growth doubled from 5% in Q2 to 9% in Q3. Loyalty revenue growth also accelerated slightly. Main Cabin trends turned positive, compared to softness previously discussed.
  • Management's confidence in margin expansion and premium demand has grown, and discussions of efficiency and cost control remain central.
  • Analysts shifted from questioning demand stability to exploring upside drivers in premium and loyalty, reflecting stronger near-term fundamentals.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited potential impacts from the ongoing U.S. government shutdown but noted "we have not seen a material effect to date."
  • Supply chain inflation in maintenance and parts remains a challenge, with Janki stating "it's got a long ways to go."
  • Atlantic RASM was flagged as disappointing, with corrective steps planned for next year.
  • Analysts raised concerns about sustainability of premium demand and the normalization of Main Cabin trends, as well as international revenue recovery.

Final Takeaway

Delta’s management reiterated its focus on profitable growth, margin expansion, and disciplined capital allocation, emphasizing the power of its premium, loyalty, and corporate revenue streams. With robust guidance for both earnings and free cash flow, continued operational reliability, and a strategic tilt toward premium product and loyalty expansion, Delta positions itself for industry-leading financial performance into the final quarter of the year and beyond.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: GM brings back the Chevy Bolt for under $30K
data.description:
General Motor’s (NYSE:GM) revived Chevy Bolt EV will be the lowest-priced electric vehicle on the market when the 2027 model is launched early next year.

At an event for Bolt owners on Wednesday, the carmaker unveiled the new crossover with a price tag under $30K with an even cheaper base model at under $29K coming later in 2026.

Chevy shelved the Bolt in 2023 even though the vehicle was one of the most popular non-Tesla EVs. The vehicle faced an extended recall, and was built on GM's (NYSE:GM) first-generation EV platform rather than the more advanced Ultium platform, leading the company to move onto other models. Public outcry encouraged the Bolt's revival, now with a refreshed front fascia, 11.3-inch touchscreen, and 11-inch digital gauge cluster.

The race for the cheapest EV has accelerated recently as EV makers manage lackluster demand magnified by an end to a $7,500 government tax credit. Detroit’s Ford (F) and GM (NYSE:GM) will continue to offer incentives to drive demand, while the top-selling EV manufacturer, Tesla (TSLA), introduced a cheaper version of its popular Model Y for under $40K.

According to details disclosed by media sources, the new Bolt’s lithium iron phosphate battery cells will have a range of 255 miles, recharge 10% to 80% in 26 minutes, with a peak charging rate of 150 kilowatts, up from 55 kW for the current Bolt.

data.title: Netgear shares rise amid report Trump is considering action against TP-Link
data.description:
• Netgear (NASDAQ:NTGR) shares rose more than 6% on Thursday after Bloomberg reported the Trump administration is considering action against Chinese router maker TP-Link Systems.
  • The administration is discussing whether to issue an “initial determination” that TP-Link could be a national security threat, the news outlet added, citing people familiar with the matter. If that happens, TP-Link systems could be restricted or outright banned in the U.S.
  • TP-Link and Netgear did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion

data.title: Ovid spikes as Wedbush moves to Outperform on lead asset
data.description:
Ovid Therapeutics (NASDAQ:OVID) added ~13% on Thursday after Wedbush revised its rating to Outperform from not rated, citing the neuro drug developer’s lead candidate OV329 targeted at rare and treatment-resistant forms of epilepsy and seizures.

Analyst Jay Olson’s bullish view on the New York-based biotech comes days after the company reported initial data from a Phase 1 trial for OV329 last week, indicating a statistically significant inhibition of GABA-AT, a target for antiepilepsy treatments.

Olson issued a 12–18-month price target of $7 on OVID, projecting over a $1B market opportunity for OV329, as he noted that the oral capsule, designed to modulate GABA receptors to treat seizures, appears to have several underappreciated advantages over rival therapies.

“Ph1 results now demonstrate a favorable safety profile, including no ocular findings for doses ≤5mg, while biomarkers support potential for strong efficacy on seizure reduction,” Olson wrote about the Phase 1 readout, which focused on 1 mg – 5 mg dose levels of OV329.

The analyst added that data for 7 mg doses expected in Q1 2026 could be a key catalyst for the stock and boost the company’s confidence as it plans a Phase 2 trial for the once-daily therapy. With the company's cash runway extending into 2028, Olson cited a compelling buying opportunity and argued that Ovid’s (NASDAQ:OVID) earlier-stage assets could generate additional upside thanks to near-term milestones.

data.title: Tech Voices: Intel's U.S. chips, Sora, Google's Schmidt on killer AI
data.description:
Seeking Alpha's roundup of statements, announcements, or remarks that could impact markets, sectors, or individual stocks. 

  • Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) unveiled its new Panther Lake chip, the first built on its 18A process technology, which it called "the most advanced semiconductor process ever developed and manufactured in the United States."

The company also previewed its Clearwater Forest 18A-based server chip, which it expects to launch in the first half of 2026. 

Both chips, along with other 18A-based products, will be produced at Fab52, Intel's new manufacturing plant in Chandler, Arizona. The plant is expected to reach high-volume production later this year.

“Our next-gen compute platforms, combined with our leading-edge process technology, manufacturing and advanced packaging capabilities, are catalysts for innovation across our business as we build a new Intel," said Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan, in a statement.

"The United States has always been home to Intel’s most advanced R&D, product design and manufacturing – and we are proud to build on this legacy as we expand our domestic operations and bring new innovations to the market," he added.

  • OpenAI (OPENAI) said its new AI video app, Sora, hit one million downloads within five days of its launch in late September.

"Sora hit 1M app downloads in <5 days, even faster than chatgpt did (despite the invite flow and only targeting north america!)! team working hard to keep up with surging growth. more features and fixes to overmoderation on the way!," said Bill Peebles, who heads up OpenAI's Sora team, in a post on X late Wednesday.

  • Former Google (NASDAQ:GOOG) (NASDAQ:GOOGL) CEO Eric Schmidt is warning that AI models can be hacked by bad actors and manipulated to harm people. 

“There’s evidence that you can take models, closed or open, and you can hack them to remove their guardrails. So in the course of their training, they learn a lot of things. A bad example would be they learn how to kill someone,” Schmidt said at an event on Wednesday, according to CNBC.

Schmidt added, however, that he is still optimistic about the technology and its potential impact on society.

“Now the GPT series, which culminated in a ChatGPT moment for all of us, where they had 100 million users in two months, which is extraordinary, gives you a sense of the power of this technology. So I think it’s underhyped, not overhyped, and I look forward to being proven correct in five or 10 years,” Schmidt said.

data.title: Break-even employment number drops, Dallas Fed says
data.description:
The number of new jobs needed to absorb the net monthly increase in U.S. labor force has declined due to reduced immigration and lower labor force participation, according to research from the Federal Reserve Bank of Dallas. 

"A new, high-frequency estimate of break-even employment shows a dramatic reversal in immigration flows, combined with cyclical shifts in labor force participation, has caused the monthly break-even requirement to collapse from a peak of approximately 250,000 in 2023 to about 30,000 in mid-2025," Anton Cheremukhin, a principal research economist at the Dallas Fed wrote on Thursday.

Note that in August, only about 22K jobs were added to U.S. nonfarm payrolls, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics' initial estimate. That's the most recent month's data for job creation from the government. The estimate for September nonfarm payrolls has been delayed due to the government shutdown. Revisions for June also showed weaker demand for labor, though July's numbers were revised slightly higher.

About half of the decline from 2023 to mid-2025 is attributed to a slowdown in population growth, with the rest explained by the slowdown in the labor force participation rate.

"This recalibration suggests that today’s more modest payroll gains don’t signal weakness but are consistent with a balanced labor market," Cheremukhin said.

The shifting dynamics implies a rebalancing of the importance placed on certain economic measures, he reasoned. "With payrolls and GDP growth heavily influenced by demographic factors, they have become less reliable indicators of economic slack or overheating," he said.

Instead, he sees unemployment as "a clearer and more stable signal of labor market slack than jobs data." In other words, the unemployment rate is a better indicator of labor market conditions than the increase or decrease in nonfarm payrolls.

The research also highlights the need for real-time data. "In a rapidly changing environment, the ability to construct high-frequency, comprehensive measures of fundamental inputs, such as population, is not just an academic exercise. It is critical for accurate economic analysis and sound policymaking."
